lineage: Life and Works of Rufinus with Jerome's Apology Against Rufinus.
Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.

His confession of faith is unsatisfactory. No one asked him about the Trinity, but about Origen's doctrines of the Resurrection, the origin of souls, and the salvability of Satan. As to the Resurrection and to Satan he is ambiguous. As to souls he professes ignorance.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Of the devil he thus frames his opinion:

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “We affirm also a judgment to come, in which judgment every man is to receive the due meed of his bodily life, according to that which he has done, whether good or evil.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: And, if in the case of men the reward is according to their works how much more will it be so in the case of the devil who is the universal cause of sin.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Of the devil himself our belief is that which is written in the Gospel, namely that both he and all his angels will receive as their portion the eternal fire, and with him those who do his works, that is, who become the accusers of their brethren.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If then any one denies that the devil is to be subjected to eternal fires, may he have his part with him in the eternal fire, so that he may know by experience the fact which he now denies.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I will repeat the words one by one.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “We affirm also a judgment to come, in which judgment &c.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I had determined to say nothing about verbal faults.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But, since his disciples admire the eloquence of their master, I will make one or two strictures upon it.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He had already said “a future judgment;” but, being a cautious man, he was afraid of saying simply “in which,” and therefore wrote “in which judgment;” for fear that, if he had not said “judgment” a second time, we, forgetting what had gone before, might have supplied the word “ass.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: That which he brings in afterwards “those who become the accusers of their brethren will with him have their portion in the eternal fire,” is in a style of equal beauty.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Who ever heard of ‘possessing the flames’?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I suppose that, being now a Greek, he had tried to translate himself, and that for the word κληρονομήσουσιν, which can be rendered in Latin by the single word Hæreditabunt, he said Hæreditate potientur supposing it to be something more elaborate and ornate.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: With such trifles and such improprieties of speech his whole discourse is teeming.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But to return to the meaning of his words.
